questions.question: Why do pose models regress heatmaps instead of (x, y) coordinates directly?
options: Heatmaps are cheaper to compute
Coordinate regression produces NaN
Heatmaps are required by the COCO metric

The spatial structure of a conv feature map aligns with spatial output; a Gaussian heatmap target provides a smooth loss landscape that tolerates small localisation errors, whereas direct coordinate regression is brittle and loses spatial context
questions.explanation: Regressing coordinates with MSE asks the network to reduce a 2D position to two scalars, losing the feature-map alignment that CNNs exploit. Heatmap regression gives the network a per-pixel loss that is smooth around the true location and preserves spatial priors. The empirical improvement over coordinate regression is large enough that every modern pose model uses heatmaps.

questions.question: A sports analytics app must track poses for 20+ simultaneous players at 30 fps. Which architecture best handles this crowd density, and why?
options: Bottom-up (OpenPose, HigherHRNet), because one forward pass produces all keypoints plus PAF association fields; runtime is constant regardless of person count
Top-down (HRNet, ViTPose), because each crop is independently processed and crops are fast
Top-down always, because per-person accuracy is highest regardless of crowd size
Both scale identically; choose by GPU memory
questions.explanation: Bottom-up runs one forward pass over the entire image and then groups all keypoints, so inference time does not grow with the number of people. Top-down runs the keypoint model once per detected person, so cost scales linearly with crowd size. For crowded real-time scenarios, bottom-up is the throughput leader even though top-down wins per-person accuracy in sparse scenes.

questions.question: In `gaussian_heatmap(size, cx, cy, sigma)`, the target value at pixel (x, y) is `exp(-((x-cx)^2 + (y-cy)^2) / (2*sigma^2))`. What is the effect of increasing sigma?
options: It normalises the heatmap to sum to 1
It sets the number of keypoints output by the network
It broadens the Gaussian peak, making the loss smoother and more tolerant of small localisation errors at the cost of lower spatial precision
It controls the learning rate for the keypoint head
questions.explanation: Sigma is the standard deviation of the 2D Gaussian. A small sigma concentrates the peak tightly (precise target, sharp gradient cliff near the boundary), while a larger sigma spreads the target (smoother loss landscape, more forgiving of minor mislocalisation). In practice sigma=2-3 pixels balances spatial precision with training stability for most pose benchmarks.

questions.question: In `heatmap_to_coords(heatmaps)`, the code reshapes heatmaps to `(N, K, H*W)`, calls `argmax(dim=-1)`, then computes `ys = idx // W` and `xs = idx % W`. What does the integer division and modulo recover?
options: The normalised [0, 1] coordinates after sub-pixel refinement
The (x, y) pixel coordinates by treating the flat argmax index as row*W + col — integer division recovers the row (y) and modulo recovers the column (x)
A confidence score for each keypoint
The batch dimension of the heatmap tensor
questions.explanation: Flattening the (H, W) heatmap to length H*W means a single scalar argmax encodes the 2D location as row*W + col. Integer division by W gives the row index (y coordinate) and the remainder gives the column index (x coordinate). This is the standard 1D-to-2D index unpacking used by every production pose model to extract integer keypoint locations from heatmaps.

questions.question: What are Part Affinity Fields (PAFs) and how do they enable bottom-up multi-person pose?
options: A scheduling algorithm for batching person crops

2-channel unit-vector fields encoding the direction from one keypoint to another; integrating the PAF along a candidate limb line scores how likely two keypoints belong to the same instance, turning pose grouping into bipartite matching
A data augmentation technique for small datasets
A type of loss function that penalises incorrect keypoint order
questions.explanation: Per connected keypoint pair (limb), predict a 2-channel field whose value at each pixel is the unit vector pointing from one keypoint (e.g. shoulder) to the other (elbow). To associate a candidate shoulder with a candidate elbow, integrate the PAF along the line joining them; higher integral = stronger match. This turns pose grouping into a bipartite matching problem solvable in polynomial time without per-person crops.

questions.question: Why does sub-pixel refinement around the argmax meaningfully improve keypoint accuracy?
options: It smooths the heatmap before loss computation
It prevents overfitting by adding regularisation

Integer argmax rounds to the nearest grid cell; fitting a local parabola or using the offset dx = 0.25*(heatmap[y,x+1] - heatmap[y,x-1]) recovers the continuous peak position, often halving L2 error for cleanly predicted keypoints
It normalises the output to [0, 1]
questions.explanation: A well-predicted heatmap has a smooth Gaussian peak whose true centre is usually between grid cells. Integer argmax introduces up to 0.5 px quantisation error. Fitting a parabola or using the first-difference offset `0.25 * (h[y,x+1] - h[y,x-1])` recovers the continuous peak. For sports analytics, medical landmarks, or any task requiring sub-pixel precision, this post-processing step is mandatory.
